title: Binary, hex, casting and the modulo operation
_comment: See decks/W1C2.json for the format and the reasoning behind it.
Reconstructed slide-for-slide from the retired LCS deck
W1C3Digitalpracticeconv.pdf (23 slides, of which 3 were per-term
announcements and are deliberately not carried over).
Same principle as W1C2: class time is for PRACTICE, so this deck does
not re-project the pre-class reading pages. The hand-worked conversion
drills are 'prompt' slides with the answer in the note, so they can be
revealed by talking rather than by advancing. The worked examples the
book already contains (2's complement, hex, and the 0xB5A9 example that
covers hex representation of NEGATIVE numbers) are pulled in with
'focus' on a single subsection - those subsections were given xml:ids
in source/digitalrep/integers.ptx for exactly this purpose.
The casting and modulo slides at the end are 'demo' slides: the LCS
originals were a line of framing plus one Dash coding window, which is

slides.title: Review: binary place values
items: Each place is a power of two: 128   64   32   16   8   4   2   1
Left-most bit is the most significant, right-most the least significant
Only the places holding a 1 contribute
8 bits = 1 byte, so an 8-bit unsigned number runs 0 to 255

slides.title: Practice: decimal → binary
slides.note: Divide by 2 repeatedly and read the remainders bottom to top. Answer: 11011110

slides.title: Practice: binary addition
slides.body: Add the decimal numbers 177 and 23 in 8-bit binary.

slides.title: Now try this one
slides.note: Recall that 177 is 10110001. Does your result make sense?

slides.title: Negative numbers
slides.body: What are the ways to represent negative numbers in binary?
slides.note: Signed magnitude — and binary 2's complement, which is what C actually uses.

slides.title: Practice: 2's complement
slides.body: Calculate 110 − 77 in binary 2's complement — that is, perform the addition 110 + (−77).

slides.title: Your turn
slides.body: Convert the decimal number 20,000 into hexadecimal.
slides.note: Keep dividing by 16 and read the remainders in reverse. Answer: 0x4E20

slides.title: Recall: integer division
slides.body: Remember the funny division behaviour we noticed in C. Run this and look at the answer:

slides.title: Casting: int to float
body: To get a decimal answer we have to tell C to treat the integers as floats. That is a cast: (float) aint.
Watch what each of the three versions below prints — especially the middle one.

slides.title: Casting: float to int
body: What happens when you cast the other way, from a float to an int? Will it round?
Write a couple of lines to find out.

slides.title: The modulo operation
slides.body: Suppose you want the remainder of the division of one integer by another. Can we do that with what we know so far?

slides.title: What we covered today
items: Three ways to read a binary number: unsigned, signed magnitude, 2's complement
Hexadecimal as shorthand for binary — and how negatives look in hex
Casting between data types: ave = ((float) a)/((float) b);
